name: Stablecon
edition: Stablecon EMEA 2026 (Amsterdam) is the edition falling inside the June 2025-June 2026 window. The original/flagship Stablecon NYC 2025 (May 29, 2025) sits just BEFORE the window but is included as context and is the source of the most detailed DeFi-panel content.
location: In-window edition: IPG Mediabrands Netherlands, 7 Peter van Anrooystraat, Amsterdam, Noord-Holland 1076 DA, Netherlands. (Original Stablecon NYC 2025: Metropolitan Pavilion, 125 W 18th St, New York, NY 10011, USA.)
dates: Stablecon EMEA 2026: May 19-20, 2026 (in window). Stablecon NYC 2025: May 29, 2025 (just before the window). For reference, the next edition is Stablecon USA 2026: September 9-11, 2026, National Harbor Resort, Washington DC (after the window).
attendance: Conflicting figures. Promotional materials for both editions claim 1,500+ attendees, 750+ C-suite leaders, and 100-125+ speakers. Independent sources are lower: a Turnkey recap of Stablecon NYC 2025 cited 460+ attendees, and an Ark Republic recap of Stablecon EMEA 2026 cited 'nearly 600 registrants.' The EMEA 2026 published speaker list contains roughly 100 named speakers, consistent with the 100+ speaker claim.
defi_themes: Convergence of TradFi and crypto/DeFi - banks and institutions integrating stablecoins rather than competing ('the future of finance was not crypto vs institutions, it was their convergence')
Stablecoins as programmable global payment rails and 'invisible financial infrastructure' rather than speculative crypto assets
Yield-bearing stablecoins and yield embedded 'under the hood' of fintech apps (typically 4-8% USD-denominated yield)
Real-World Asset (RWA) tokenization, tokenized Treasuries, tokenized bonds and collateral, and private credit as the bridge between TradFi and DeFi
On-chain / enterprise treasury operations and corporate adoption of stablecoins for cash management
Institutional DeFi and programmable money enabling new capital-formation and lending models
Stablecoin regulation as a product enabler - MiCA (EU), UK stablecoin frameworks, US policy, and Middle East/Africa regulatory approaches
Cross-border payments and settlement - reducing multi-country transfers from days to minutes
Liquidity automation, multi-chain interoperability, and bridge/cross-chain infrastructure ('infrastructure built to bend')
Local-currency / non-dollar stablecoins and monetary sovereignty (notably emerging markets and Africa)
Custodial vs non-custodial key management and the regulatory grey areas of non-custodial DeFi solutions
Commoditization of stablecoin issuance and competitive pressure on net-interest-margin business models

notable_panels.title: The Stablecoin Regulatory Map
notable_panels.topic: Regulatory frameworks across regions, compliance challenges, and fragmented global policy for stablecoins (Stablecon NYC 2025)
notable_panels.speakers: Moderator: Laura Sanders (Senior Counsel, Blockchain Association); Sinclair Toffa (Co-founder/CEO, MuralPay); Kevin Lehtiniitty (CEO, Borderless.xyz)

notable_panels.title: Shipping Stablecoins
notable_panels.topic: Technical infrastructure, build-vs-buy decisions, and custodial vs non-custodial wallets (Stablecon NYC 2025)
notable_panels.speakers: Moderator: Arnaud Brousseau (Founding Engineer, Turnkey); Chuk Okpalugo (Product Leader, Paxos); Drake Evans (Co-founder/CTO, Agora)

notable_panels.title: The Stablecoin Economy
notable_panels.topic: Revenue models, issuance/distribution, and value distribution across ecosystem layers; yield-bearing assets and embedded fintech models (Stablecon NYC 2025)
notable_panels.speakers: Moderator: Hannah Arnold (COO, Turnkey); Mike Giampapa (General Partner, Galaxy); Greg Prisco (Co-founder, M0)

notable_panels.title: Accelerating Stablecoin Infrastructure
notable_panels.topic: Stablecoins as foundational finance infrastructure and global money movement ('it is materially cheaper to build on stablecoins; you get a ledger free') (Stablecon NYC 2025)
notable_panels.speakers: Moderator: Bryce Ferguson (CEO, Turnkey); Zach Abrams (CEO/Co-founder, Bridge)

notable_panels.title: Monetary sovereignty / local-currency stablecoins panel (Stablecon EMEA 2026)
notable_panels.topic: Non-dollar stablecoins, monetary sovereignty, and stablecoin adoption in Africa and emerging markets. Note: exact session title was not published; reconstructed from the Ark Republic EMEA 2026 recap.
notable_panels.speakers: Mimi Kufuor (KoinKoin); Jannah Patchay (Global Digital Finance); Kene Ezeji-Okoye (Ubyx); Kwadwo Botwe (Bank of Ghana)

key_takeaways: The framing of stablecoins has fundamentally shifted from speculative crypto tools to institutional financial infrastructure ('invisible financial infrastructure' / programmable global payment rails); the dominant theme was TradFi-DeFi convergence rather than competition.

Regulatory clarity is the key unlock for institutional adoption. MiCA (EU), UK frameworks, and US policy are treated as product enablers ('policy is product'), while non-custodial DeFi solutions still operate in regulatory grey areas.

Yield is moving on-chain and embedded: builders increasingly ship fintech-style apps with yield-bearing stablecoins 'under the hood,' and RWA tokenization (Treasuries, bonds, private credit) is the bridge solving the 'dormant collateral' problem and connecting TradFi to DeFi.

Stablecoin issuance is commoditizing, pressuring net-interest-margin models; the predicted end state is a layered ecosystem where branded stablecoins (USDC, USDT) coexist with proprietary platform/enterprise coins, with value capture shifting up the stack to distribution and applications.

Cross-border payments and emerging markets are the killer use case - especially Africa, where stablecoins address correspondent-banking inefficiencies and currency volatility; local-currency / non-dollar stablecoins and monetary sovereignty were major EMEA 2026 threads even as dollar dominance persists.

The primary bottleneck to adoption is educational, not technical (per Zach Abrams of Bridge); infrastructure must remain abstracted from end users, and enterprise adoption (treasury, B2B payments) is expected to drive growth more than consumer demand.
